items.title: Kyoto in the Rain
items.content:
It rained the entire week I spent in Kyoto, and I cannot recommend it highly enough. The city is built for weather — the deep eaves of the wooden machiya houses, the moss that drinks light off the stones at Saiho-ji, the steam curling off bowls of udon in basement noodle bars where the windows fog from the inside.

Fushimi Inari at dawn

I climbed through the vermilion gates at five in the morning, alone except for a single shrine cat who watched me with the polite suspicion of a customs officer. The torii recede uphill into mist, each one inscribed with the name of a donor, each one a small act of faith stacked on top of the last.

A garden in Kyoto is a sentence. The rain is the punctuation.

What to pack for a wet week

  • A genuinely waterproof jacket — not 'water resistant', not 'showerproof'.
  • Two pairs of shoes, so one can dry while the other works.
  • A small folding umbrella; the convenience-store ones snap by Tuesday.
  • A notebook with a hard cover. Soft covers warp.

The kissaten on a side street

Find the old coffee shops. Not the third-wave ones with the V60s and the Aesop hand soap, but the kissaten — dim, wood-panelled rooms where a man in a bow tie has been making the same siphon coffee since 1974 and where the cheesecake comes on a doily. Sit by the window. Watch the rain. Don't look at your phone.

Etiquette note

Slurp the noodles. Don't tip. Take your shoes off when the floor changes level. When in doubt, do what the person next to you is doing.

A loose itinerary

  1. Day 1 — Arrive, eat tonkatsu, sleep early.
  2. Day 2 — Fushimi Inari at dawn, Tofukuji in the afternoon.
  3. Day 3 — Philosopher's Path, end at Honen-in.
  4. Day 4 — Day trip to Uji for the matcha and the tea museum.
  5. Day 5 — Nishiki Market, then a long evening in a kissaten.

Leaving

I left Kyoto on the bullet train with damp shoes and the strong sense that I had only seen a tenth of it. That, I think, is the correct way to leave.

aemBlocks.html: It rained the entire week I spent in Kyoto, and I cannot recommend it highly enough. The city is built for weather — the deep eaves of the wooden machiya houses, the moss that drinks light off the stones at Saiho-ji, the steam curling off bowls of udon in basement noodle bars where the windows fog from the inside.

I climbed through the vermilion gates at five in the morning, alone except for a single shrine cat who watched me with the polite suspicion of a customs officer. The torii recede uphill into mist, each one inscribed with the name of a donor, each one a small act of faith stacked on top of the last.

items.title: Lost on Purpose in the Marrakech Souks
items.content:
There is a particular kind of peace that arrives only after you have completely abandoned the hope of finding your hotel. The Marrakech medina is engineered for this. Streets fold into streets, doorways open into courtyards that open into other courtyards, and the call to prayer ricochets off pink walls in a way that makes triangulation impossible.

The colour of everything

Saffron, indigo, tagine-orange, the deep pomegranate red of Berber rugs hung against ochre plaster — Marrakech is a city that doesn't believe in beige. The dyers' souk is the most photographed alley in the medina for a reason: skeins of wool the colour of poured paint, drying overhead like bunting.

If you can find your way out, you weren't really lost.

How to actually haggle

  1. Decide what the object is worth to you before you ask the price.
  2. Counter at roughly a third. Smile while you do it.
  3. Be willing to walk away once. Often that's when the real price appears.
  4. If you agree on a number, you buy. Walking away after is bad form.

Mint tea, properly

Accept every cup. The pour is the performance — held high so the tea aerates into the glass with a small, contented hiss. It will be sweeter than you want. Drink it anyway. Three glasses, the saying goes: the first bitter as life, the second sweet as love, the third gentle as death.

A small kindness

Learn five words of Darija before you go. 'Shukran' alone will change how a whole afternoon treats you.

What to bring home

  • A hand-hammered brass tea tray (check the rim is even).
  • Saffron from the spice souk, sealed in foil, not glass.
  • One small Beni Ourain rug, rolled tight, checked as luggage.
  • Argan oil — culinary grade, not the cosmetic stuff.

Streets fold into streets, doorways open into courtyards that open into other courtyards, and the call to prayer ricochets off pink walls in a way that makes triangulation impossible.

Saffron, indigo, tagine-orange, the deep pomegranate red of Berber rugs hung against ochre plaster — Marrakech is a city that doesn't believe in beige. The dyers' souk is the most photographed alley in the medina for a reason: skeins of wool the colour of poured paint, drying overhead like bunting.

items.excerpt: A guide to surrendering your sense of direction in the world's most beautifully disorienting marketplace.

items.title: Patagonia: Notes from the End of the World
items.content:
The wind in Patagonia is a character, not a condition. It has moods. It enters rooms. It bullies the laundry on the line behind the hostel and it changes its mind about which direction to come from with the petulance of a small monarch. I spent eleven days in El Chaltén and I never once stopped noticing it.

Walking toward Fitz Roy

The trail up to Laguna de los Tres begins as a friendly stroll through lenga forest and ends, four hours later, as a near-vertical scramble across scree that punishes everyone equally. The reward is one of the great views on the planet: the granite needles of Fitz Roy and Cerro Torre rising out of a milk-blue glacial lake, sharp as cut paper against the sky.

1,150 m
Total ascent
22 km
Round trip
9–11 hrs
Time on the trail
Always one more
Layers required
Mountains do not require you. That is the entire appeal.

Packing for the wind

  • A hard-shell jacket with a hood that actually cinches.
  • Gloves you can still tie a knot in.
  • Sunglasses — the glacier light is brutal even on grey days.
  • Twice the snacks you think you need; the wind eats calories.

The town at the trailhead

El Chaltén feels improvised, in the best way — a settlement of climbers, bakers, and dogs that all seem to know each other. The bread at La Cervecería is worth the walk across town. So is the beer. Sit on the patio when the wind drops, which it sometimes does, around the time the alpenglow hits the peaks and the whole valley goes briefly, ridiculously pink.

If you only have one day

Walk to Laguna Capri and back. Four hours, gentle, and you still get the Fitz Roy view through a frame of lenga branches.

Going home

I came home with a sunburn on one cheek, a permanently chapped lower lip, and the unshakeable conviction that everyone should, at least once, stand somewhere the wind has been blowing since long before there was anyone here to lean into it.

aemBlocks.html: The wind in Patagonia is a character, not a condition. It has moods. It enters rooms. It bullies the laundry on the line behind the hostel and it changes its mind about which direction to come from with the petulance of a small monarch. I spent eleven days in El Chaltén and I never once stopped noticing it.

The trail up to Laguna de los Tres begins as a friendly stroll through lenga forest and ends, four hours later, as a near-vertical scramble across scree that punishes everyone equally. The reward is one of the great views on the planet: the granite needles of Fitz Roy and Cerro Torre rising out of a milk-blue glacial lake, sharp as cut paper against the sky.

Total ascent
22 km

9–11 hrs

Always one more
Layers required
